questions.question: Define sample space and give an example for the experiment of drawing a card from a deck of 52 cards.
keyPoints: Sample space is the set of all possible outcomes
Contains 52 equally likely outcomes

questions.question: State and prove the addition rule of probability for two events.
keyPoints: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B)
Proof uses Venn diagram or set theory
For mutually exclusive: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B)

questions.question: What is conditional probability? If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A ∩ B) = 0.2, find P(A|B).
keyPoints: Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B)
Interpretation: Probability of A given B has occurred

questions.question: Explain the difference between mutually exclusive and independent events with examples.
keyPoints: Mutually exclusive: Cannot occur together (P(A ∩ B) = 0)
Independent: One does not affect other (P(A|B) = P(A))
Example: Rolling die - {2} and {4} are mutually exclusive, not independent

questions.question: A bag contains 6 white and 4 black balls. Two balls are drawn at random. Find probability that both are white (a) with replacement (b) without replacement.
keyPoints: Without replacement: P = (6/10) × (5/9) = 30/90 = 1/3
Replacement affects second draw probability

questions.question: State Bayes' theorem and explain its significance in computing applications.
keyPoints: Bayes' theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B)
Used in spam filtering, medical diagnosis, machine learning
Updates prior probability with new evidence to get posterior

questions.question: A computer system has two components: A fails with probability 0.1, B fails with probability 0.2, and both fail simultaneously with probability 0.05. Find probability that (i) at least one works (ii) exactly one fails.
keyPoints: P(at least one works) = 1 - P(both fail) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
P(exactly one fails) = P(A fails and B works) + P(A works and B fails)

questions.question: A factory produces items on three machines A, B, C producing 50%, 30%, 20% of total output respectively. The defect rates are 2%, 3%, and 5% respectively. If a randomly selected item is defective, find probability it was produced by machine C using Bayes' theorem.
keyPoints: Define events: D = defective, A/B/C = produced by respective machines

questions.question: Derive the formula for probability of union of three events: P(A ∪ B ∪ C). A computer network has three servers. The probability they are down is 0.1, 0.2, and 0.15 respectively. Assuming independence, find probability that at least one server is working.
keyPoints: Formula: P(A∪B∪C) = P(A)+P(B)+P(C) - P(A∩B) - P(B∩C) - P(A∩C) + P(A∩B∩C)
For at least one working: use complement
P(at least one working) = 1 - 0.003 = 0.997
Or compute: 1 - P(all three fail)

questions.question: Explain classical, relative frequency, and subjective approaches to probability. In a simulation of 10000 random password attempts, 347 were successful. Estimate probability of success and explain which approach this represents.
keyPoints: Classical: Based on equally likely outcomes (theoretical)
Relative frequency: Based on past observations (empirical)
Subjective: Based on personal judgment (Bayesian)
Here: 347/10000 = 0.0347 represents relative frequency approach
As n→∞, relative frequency approaches true probability (law of large numbers)

questions.question: Three events A, B, C in a sample space have probabilities P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.3, P(C)=0.2, P(A∩B)=0.1, P(B∩C)=0.05, P(A∩C)=0.08, P(A∩B∩C)=0.03. Find: (i) P(A∪B) (ii) P(A∪C) (iii) P(A∪B∪C).
keyPoints: Verify: Using addition rule for three events
